articles.shortSummary: Ketamine remains a workhorse in pediatric ED sedation, so a large adverse-event review is immediately relevant even if it does not produce a dramatic new warning.
articles.summary: Ketamine remains a workhorse in pediatric ED sedation, so a large adverse-event review is immediately relevant even if it does not produce a dramatic new warning. The strength here is aggregation: pooled event rates and a better sense of which proposed predictors actually deserve clinician attention. That should help departments counsel families, refine checklists, and calibrate how much weight to place on risk factors that are often repeated more confidently than they are proven. Expect this to be more protocol-shaping than practice-reversing.

articles.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
articles.takeaway: Treat this as research infrastructure rather than bedside guidance. The immediate practical message is that early sepsis trial design is improving, not that albumin has earned a routine ED role.
articles.shortSummary: This is less a sepsis practice changer than a trial-design note from the ICARUS-ED orbit.
articles.summary: This is less a sepsis practice changer than a trial-design note from the ICARUS-ED orbit. Its value is in showing how difficult early ED sepsis intervention studies are to run well and where future albumin trials may need to tighten design, inclusion, and endpoints. For clinicians, the paper is interesting because it reminds us how often feasibility questions shape what evidence we eventually get. It is useful background if you follow sepsis fluids closely, but it should not be over-read as a mandate for albumin use.

articles.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
articles.takeaway: Medication review in older adults should be an active intervention, not a documentation task. If you can hard-wire pharmacy support or prescribing prompts into discharge workflow, you will likely prevent more harm than with another generic frailty slogan.
articles.shortSummary: Older adults continue to pay the price for fragmented medication histories, and this review makes that point without much ambiguity.
articles.summary: Older adults continue to pay the price for fragmented medication histories, and this review makes that point without much ambiguity. The most actionable themes are thorough reconciliation, deliberate deprescribing of inappropriate agents, and building pharmacist or decision-support input into ED workflows. None of that is glamorous, but medication harm remains one of the most fixable drivers of poor geriatric outcomes after discharge. If your department wants a high-yield systems target, this is one.

articles.title: TTL Tip 11: Be the TTL your prehospital teams want to hand over to
articles.takeaway: If you lead trauma calls, build the handover before arrival: anticipate interventions, frame the priorities, and protect the first minute from chaos. ATMIST is only useful if the receiving team is actually ready to hear it.
articles.shortSummary: This is FOAMed, but it addresses a real performance gap: poor trauma handovers waste time precisely when teams need shared situational awareness.
articles.summary: This is FOAMed, but it addresses a real performance gap: poor trauma handovers waste time precisely when teams need shared situational awareness. The best point is that good handover starts before wheels hit the bay, with the trauma leader shaping the room, the roles, and the expected problems from the pre-alert. That sounds obvious, yet it is often the difference between an orderly reception and noisy improvisation. Operational advice like this travels well because it is immediately testable on shift.

articles.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
articles.takeaway: Digital tools may help close testing gaps if they slot cleanly into registration or waiting-room flow. The right benchmark is not novelty but whether they improve uptake without creating new bottlenecks.
articles.shortSummary: This trial sits in the growing category of digital nudges that try to improve preventive or public-health work inside the ED without overloading staff.
articles.summary: This trial sits in the growing category of digital nudges that try to improve preventive or public-health work inside the ED without overloading staff. A patient-facing STI decision tool could matter if it increases testing uptake while preserving autonomy and workflow. The main question is less whether the concept is clever and more whether the effect size justifies implementation friction in a busy department. Still, this is the sort of intervention that can quietly outperform posters and vague clinician prompts.

articles.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
articles.takeaway: Do not write off moderate-to-severe MeVO patients as automatically outside the thrombectomy conversation. Early specialist discussion and transfer logic may need to be more inclusive as this evidence matures.
articles.shortSummary: Medium-vessel occlusion is starting to look less like a no-man’s-land for reperfusion decisions.
articles.summary: Medium-vessel occlusion is starting to look less like a no-man’s-land for reperfusion decisions. The ORIENTAL-MeVO report suggests thrombectomy can improve functional independence in selected patients with meaningful deficits, extending the scope of who might benefit from advanced stroke pathways. As always, the exciting part is balanced by questions of external validity, service capability, and patient selection. But it is one more sign that stroke systems may keep widening beyond classic large-vessel targets.

articles.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
articles.takeaway: Rapidly progressive weakness with autonomic involvement and a sensory level is a spinal emergency until proven otherwise. Prioritize localization, urgent imaging, and early specialty escalation rather than getting lost in broad weakness workups.
articles.shortSummary: This clinical image-style case looks like a reminder of how quickly a spinal cord process can announce itself if you pay attention to pattern recognition.
articles.summary: This clinical image-style case looks like a reminder of how quickly a spinal cord process can announce itself if you pay attention to pattern recognition. Rapid bilateral weakness, sphincter dysfunction, and a sensory level are the kind of constellation that should pull attention away from peripheral causes and toward urgent cord localization. The educational value is probably in the differential and the need for decisive imaging rather than in any exotic diagnosis. Cases like this are useful because they sharpen tempo-and-pattern instincts.

articles.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
articles.takeaway: Shorter prehospital timelines are a worthy systems goal, but interpret time associations cautiously. The better question is which delays are modifiable without stripping away necessary on-scene care.
articles.shortSummary: PATOS reinforces the old truth that time matters in trauma, but also the equally important truth that sicker patients consume more time.
articles.summary: PATOS reinforces the old truth that time matters in trauma, but also the equally important truth that sicker patients consume more time. Longer scene and resuscitation intervals tracked with worse outcomes, yet the authors appropriately flag confounding by severity. That means the paper is most useful for system reflection rather than simplistic stopwatch medicine. It supports streamlining prehospital care while resisting the lazy conclusion that every extra minute is avoidable error.

articles.title: Major adverse cardiac events in patients with indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin testing and chest pain: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: Do not over-interpret an isolated indeterminate hs-troponin. Serial testing, ECG context, and overall risk profile still do most of the real clinical work.
articles.shortSummary: Indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin results remain one of the more annoying modern chest pain problems, and this review confirms that the evidence around them is still messy.
articles.summary: Indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin results remain one of the more annoying modern chest pain problems, and this review confirms that the evidence around them is still messy. Many patients in the literature did not even get serial testing, so it is hard to know what risk we are really measuring. That makes the paper less a rule-set and more a warning against false precision. In other words, an awkward troponin should push you back toward clinical assessment, not trick you into pretending the lab has settled the case.

articles.title: High-Risk Medication Prescribing Among Older Adults in the Emergency Department: A National Assessment
articles.takeaway: Improving geriatric prescribing is not just about the very old or obviously frail. Build discharge safeguards broadly, and check whether your own prescribing patterns mirror the subgroups flagged here.
articles.shortSummary: Nearly one in ten older adults filling a high-risk discharge prescription is still a substantial systems failure, even if the trend line is improving.
articles.summary: Nearly one in ten older adults filling a high-risk discharge prescription is still a substantial systems failure, even if the trend line is improving. The signal that younger older adults and women were more exposed is useful because it identifies groups who may slip past generic geriatric safeguards. This is not an abstract prescribing-quality paper; it is a reminder that discharge is where a lot of medication harm gets exported out of the ED. There is enough here to justify targeted audit and feedback.

articles.title: Civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury in South-East Norway
articles.takeaway: Penetrating TBI should not trigger automatic therapeutic pessimism in salvageable patients. Early CTA, guideline-concordant neurocritical care, and prompt operative planning remain the basics worth getting right.
articles.shortSummary: This series is valuable because it combines a grim injury pattern with a fairly practical message: survivors can do well enough that nihilism is a mistake, but care quality still depends on disciplined adherence to TBI…
articles.summary: This series is valuable because it combines a grim injury pattern with a fairly practical message: survivors can do well enough that nihilism is a mistake, but care quality still depends on disciplined adherence to TBI principles. Lower-than-expected compliance with antibiotics, seizure prophylaxis, ICP monitoring, and timely revision highlights how easily even high-stakes trauma care drifts from guideline intent. The call for aggressive management in potential survivors feels justified. For trauma teams, this reads as both outcome data and a process audit.

editions.label: 20 May 2026
editions.headline: Fluid Choice, Trauma Resuscitation, and the Evolving Algorithm
editions.summary:
The literature this week presents several high-yield shifts, particularly concerning resuscitation protocols. In pediatric septic shock, the PRoMPT BOLUS trial offers reassuring data, suggesting that for initial fluid boluses, the choice between 0.9% saline and balanced crystalloids does not impact major outcomes like kidney injury or mortality. This finding grants considerable latitude in fluid management protocols.

Shifting focus to trauma, the TOWAR trial challenges the historical emphasis on whole blood products en route to the hospital; for anticipated hemorrhagic shock, the data suggests that component therapy provides comparable outcomes to whole blood, emphasizing that the overall resuscitation strategy remains paramount.

Beyond resuscitation, procedural efficiency is gaining traction. For pediatric patients struggling with peripheral IV access, using a liquid crystal vein locator appears to offer a statistically significant boost in first-attempt success rates. Meanwhile, in the realm of pulmonary embolism, the HI-PEITHO trial keeps the discussion alive regarding the utility of ultrasound-guided, catheter-directed fibrinolysis for intermediate-risk patients, urging careful risk-benefit assessment.

These reads collectively underscore a theme: the evidence is increasingly refining the 'best practice' from rigid dogma to context-specific, evidence-based algorithms, whether it's in fluid choice, blood product selection, or procedural adjuncts.
editions.notes: Keep questioning the 'standard' approach when the data suggests equivalence or a more nuanced pathway.

articles.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: While the theoretical benefits of lower nurse-to-patient ratios are compelling, this review highlights that the evidence linking specific ratios directly to reduced ED mortality is not conclusive. When considering staffing adjustments, focus on process improvements and acuity-based staffing models rather than relying solely on a fixed numerical ratio, as the literature suggests this correlation is complex and context-dependent.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review tackles the perennial question of whether implementing formal patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department translates into measurable improvements for patient outcomes, staff well-being, an…
articles.summary: This systematic review tackles the perennial question of whether implementing formal patient-to-nurse ratios in the emergency department translates into measurable improvements for patient outcomes, staff well-being, and overall departmental function. While the concept of ratio-based staffing is well-established in other acute care settings, its applicability and impact specifically within the dynamic environment of the ED remains somewhat nebulous. The authors synthesized data from studies published between 2004 and 2025 to assess the association between these ratios and mortality rates. Overall, the review suggests that while some evidence points toward benefits, the current body of literature is insufficient or inconsistent to provide definitive recommendations for mandatory ratio implementation in the ED setting.

articles.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
articles.takeaway: When managing a trauma patient anticipated to enter hemorrhagic shock, the choice between whole blood versus component therapy en route appears to be equally effective based on this data. You can continue to use your established protocol, but this trial suggests that the theoretical benefit of whole blood over components may not translate to improved outcomes at the bedside. Always remember that resuscitation remains a dynamic process, and the underlying cause of hemorrhage dictates the ultimate management.
articles.shortSummary: The TOWAR trial provides data suggesting that for patients presenting with a high risk of hemorrhagic shock following trauma, the initial transfusion of whole, type O blood does not confer a survival advantage over admi…
articles.summary: The TOWAR trial provides data suggesting that for patients presenting with a high risk of hemorrhagic shock following trauma, the initial transfusion of whole, type O blood does not confer a survival advantage over administering specific blood components en route to the definitive care center. This randomized trial directly compared these two approaches in the setting of anticipated massive transfusion. The findings indicate that both strategies resulted in comparable clinical outcomes, which is a nuanced point for resuscitation protocols. This challenges the historical preference for whole blood products in the prehospital setting when managing potential hemorrhagic shock from trauma. It suggests that the composition of the transfusion product might be less critical than the overall resuscitation strategy itself.

articles.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
articles.takeaway: For intermediate-risk PE, the data from HI-PEITHO suggests considering US-CDT alongside anticoagulation if the patient profile warrants reperfusion consideration beyond standard management. However, remember that this is a procedural intervention, and its utility must be weighed against the risks of catheter-based thrombolysis. Always assess the patient's hemodynamic stability and local expertise before proceeding with this combined approach.
articles.shortSummary: The HI-PEITHO trial investigated whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes in patients presenting with intermediate-risk pulmonary embol…
articles.summary: The HI-PEITHO trial investigated whether adding ultrasound-facilitated, catheter-directed fibrinolysis (US-CDT) to standard anticoagulation improves outcomes in patients presenting with intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism (PE). This is a relevant question because intermediate-risk PE patients often fall into a gray zone regarding definitive reperfusion therapy. The trial compared the combination of US-CDT plus anticoagulation against anticoagulation alone. While the full results are what we need to digest, the focus here is on the procedural utility and safety profile of this combined approach in a setting where standard management might be insufficient.

articles.title: Effect of a liquid crystal vein locator on intravenous catheterization in young children: a randomized trial
articles.takeaway: When struggling with PIVC in a young child, incorporating a liquid crystal vein locator appears beneficial, as it statistically improves first-attempt success and reduces the overall number of sticks. While the data is compelling, remember that this is a randomized trial, and its utility should be weighed against the time cost of using the device itself. It seems like a worthwhile adjunct tool for difficult pediatric venipuncture.
articles.shortSummary: This randomized trial investigated the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator when performing peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) in young children.
articles.summary: This randomized trial investigated the utility of a liquid crystal vein locator when performing peripheral intravenous catheterization (PIVC) in young children. The authors found that utilizing this specific locator was associated with several positive outcomes compared to standard practice. Specifically, the use of the locator correlated with a significantly higher first-attempt success rate for PIVC. Furthermore, the data suggested that its use led to a reduction in the total number of puncture attempts required and decreased overall procedural times. These findings suggest a tangible benefit to the procedural efficiency and success rate when cannulating small, difficult veins in pediatric patients.

articles.title: ACMT Toxicology Visual Pearl – Sauté Gone Sour
articles.takeaway: When considering mushroom poisoning, remember that Amanita ocreata is strongly associated with significant liver failure. While other toxicities like seizures or hallucinations can occur with different agents, the primary concern here is hepatic insult. Always maintain a high index of suspicion for severe hepatotoxicity when a patient presents with unknown mushroom ingestion.
articles.shortSummary: This visual pearl focuses on the toxicity associated with ingesting the mushroom Amanita ocreata, commonly known as one of the 'destroying angels' within the Amanita genus.
articles.summary: This visual pearl focuses on the toxicity associated with ingesting the mushroom Amanita ocreata, commonly known as one of the 'destroying angels' within the Amanita genus. The core educational point is to link this specific mushroom to a particular organ system failure, which in this case is liver failure. While the format presents it as a multiple-choice question, the underlying importance is recognizing the specific hepatotoxic profile associated with this deadly species. It serves as a quick, high-yield reminder for toxicological differentials encountered in the ED setting.

articles.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
articles.takeaway: For stable patients presenting with CAP, remember that antibiotic necessity is increasingly questioned, especially if viral etiology is confirmed. Consider utilizing lung ultrasound when available, and when initiating antibiotics, aim for the shorter courses recommended, starting at a minimum of three days. Always review the patient's overall stability and risk profile before committing to full-spectrum empiric coverage.
articles.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines offer an updated perspective on the diagnosis and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in the emergency department.
articles.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines offer an updated perspective on the diagnosis and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in the emergency department. These guidelines synthesize current evidence regarding imaging, antibiotic stewardship, and the role of adjunctive therapies like corticosteroids. Notably, the recommendations support the integration of lung ultrasound by experienced personnel into the diagnostic algorithm. Furthermore, the guidelines suggest a more nuanced approach to antibiotic use, particularly for otherwise healthy outpatients who test positive for viral pathogens. Clinically, there's a push toward de-escalation, recommending shorter antibiotic courses for both stable outpatients and nonsevere inpatients.

articles.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
articles.takeaway: For initial fluid resuscitation in pediatric septic shock, the choice between 0.9% saline and balanced crystalloids appears clinically equivalent regarding major endpoints like kidney injury and mortality. This suggests that while institutional protocols may favor one over the other, the underlying physiological impact on these outcomes is not dictated by the specific crystalloid used. Always remember to tailor fluid management to the patient's hemodynamic status, but this trial supports flexibility in fluid choice.
articles.shortSummary: The PRoMPT BOLUS trial provides a large, pragmatic randomized comparison of 0.9% saline versus balanced crystalloids for initial fluid resuscitation in pediatric septic shock.
articles.summary: The PRoMPT BOLUS trial provides a large, pragmatic randomized comparison of 0.9% saline versus balanced crystalloids for initial fluid resuscitation in pediatric septic shock. This is a highly anticipated piece because fluid choice remains a point of significant clinical debate in pediatric critical care. The study's primary finding is that there was no discernible difference in major clinical outcomes, such as kidney injury or mortality, when using either fluid type. This evidence is valuable because it directly addresses the ongoing controversy surrounding fluid composition in the setting of pediatric septic shock management. Clinicians can now use this data to guide their real-world fluid resuscitation protocols.

articles.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
articles.takeaway: When faced with an undifferentiated poisoned patient, adopt a systematic toxidromic approach, focusing on the clinical picture and the expected toxicokinetics rather than waiting for a definitive diagnosis. Use this guideline to structure your initial workup and management plan, but always remember this is a general framework. Do not let it replace consulting the specific, detailed poison information available from the NPIS or TOXBASE for the suspected agent.
articles.shortSummary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a valuable, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED where poisoning is suspected…
articles.summary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a valuable, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED where poisoning is suspected but not yet confirmed. Recognizing the complexity of undifferentiated toxic ingestions, the authors advocate for a toxidromic approach, meaning the management strategy should pivot around the clinical syndrome and the suspected toxicokinetics of the agents involved. The guidance is designed to support initial assessment, aid in identifying potential toxins, and guide immediate emergency management steps. It is crucial to understand that this document is intended as a general clinical scaffold and explicitly does not supersede the detailed, poison-specific protocols found in resources like TOXBASE or the NPIS itself. This comprehensive approach helps structure care when the specific agent is unknown.

articles.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
articles.takeaway: When considering ketamine for pediatric procedural sedation, remember that pooled data are being used to estimate adverse event frequencies, which is helpful for risk assessment. While the review inventories predictors, clinicians should interpret these findings cautiously, as the actual clinical importance of any single variable remains subject to the quality and scope of the underlying studies. Always maintain vigilance for signs of adverse reactions, regardless of the established predictive profile.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department. The primary goal was to consolidate the reported rates of various adverse events associated with this anesthetic agent in this specific population. Furthermore, the authors undertook an inventory of predictor variables mentioned in the included studies to gauge their potential clinical utility. By pooling data from studies involving at least 250 pediatric subjects, the review aims to provide more robust estimates of event frequencies and to help delineate which factors might predict adverse outcomes. This synthesis is valuable for standardizing care and refining sedation protocols in the ED setting.

articles.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
articles.takeaway: Do not routinely administer IV thrombolytics to minor stroke patients simply because they present to the ED. Reserve this therapy for those with clear, measurable deficits that suggest a significant underlying pathology warranting reperfusion. Remember that the benefit needs to be tied to a specific, demonstrable impairment, not just the diagnosis of minor stroke.
articles.shortSummary: This piece tackles the somewhat murky area of using intravenous thrombolytics for patients presenting with minor stroke.
articles.summary: This piece tackles the somewhat murky area of using intravenous thrombolytics for patients presenting with minor stroke. The core message is a cautionary one, suggesting that the current evidence base doesn't support routine use of IV thrombolytics in this population. The authors argue that future randomized controlled trials need to be more granular, specifically investigating which types of neurological deficits might actually benefit from reperfusion therapy. Overall, the consensus leans toward reserving this potent intervention for cases where there is clear, measurable, and significant impairment, rather than offering it broadly to all minor stroke patients.

articles.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
articles.takeaway: When managing the elderly patient in the ED, prioritize a thorough medication reconciliation process and actively screen for potentially inappropriate drugs using validated tools. Don't hesitate to engage pharmacy services early in the workup, as systematic deprescribing is a key intervention that can improve safety without delaying necessary acute care. Remember that while these tools are helpful, clinical judgment remains essential for balancing de-prescribing with acute symptom management.
articles.shortSummary: This narrative review tackles the critical issue of medication safety in the elderly patient presenting to the emergency department, recognizing that polypharmacy and medication exposure are major risk factors in this i…
articles.summary: This narrative review tackles the critical issue of medication safety in the elderly patient presenting to the emergency department, recognizing that polypharmacy and medication exposure are major risk factors in this increasingly common demographic. Given that older adults constitute a significant portion of ED visits and often present with multiple comorbidities, optimizing their medication regimens is paramount for improving outcomes. The authors emphasize that medication reconciliation is a cornerstone of care, alongside the systematic deprescribing of potentially inappropriate medications using established tools such as Beers criteria or STOPP/START. Furthermore, the review highlights that integrating dedicated emergency medicine pharmacist support and leveraging clinical decision support systems can significantly bolster safety protocols in the acute setting.

articles.title: TTL Tip 11: Be the TTL your prehospital teams want to hand over to
articles.takeaway: When receiving a major trauma patient, ensure your handover is structured, perhaps using ATMIST, and that your team is already prepped for resuscitation before the patient arrives. Remember that proactive preparation and clear communication are as vital as the initial assessment. Don't forget to debrief the handover process itself to identify areas for improving future teamwork.
articles.shortSummary: This piece from St.
articles.summary: This piece from St. Emlyn's focuses on optimizing the transition of care for major trauma patients, specifically addressing the handover process from prehospital teams to the emergency department trauma team. It emphasizes that the trauma team leader (TTL) plays a crucial role in ensuring a smooth and effective transfer of care. The core message revolves around structuring the handover using a standardized format, such as ATMIST, and proactively preparing the resuscitation bay before the patient even arrives. Beyond just communication, the article touches on improving overall teamwork dynamics with Helicopter Emergency Medical Service (HEMS) crews and enhancing patient safety protocols during these high-stress transfers.

articles.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
articles.takeaway: The data suggests that minimizing the time spent in VF between shocks, perhaps via strategies like DSED, might be a key driver of improved OHCA outcomes. While this is based on a secondary analysis of an RCT, it points toward optimizing the rhythm management process rather than just the shock itself. Clinicians should keep DSED in mind as a potential adjunct to standard protocols, but remember this is an evolving area requiring further dedicated study.
articles.shortSummary: This review analyzes defibrillation data from the DOSE-VF Trial to determine if simply reducing the time spent in ventricular fibrillation (VF) between shocks is the mechanism driving survival benefits in out-of-hospita…
articles.summary: This review analyzes defibrillation data from the DOSE-VF Trial to determine if simply reducing the time spent in ventricular fibrillation (VF) between shocks is the mechanism driving survival benefits in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The analysis compared outcomes across different defibrillation approaches, specifically looking at Double Sequential Defibrillation (DSED) and Vector Change (VC) defibrillation against standard protocols. The key finding suggests that both DSED and VC strategies are associated with a reduction in the duration of VF between shocks, with DSED showing the most encouraging signal. This shifts the focus from just the type of shock to the overall efficiency of defibrillation cycles in the field setting.

articles.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
articles.takeaway: If you are concerned about optimizing STI screening in your ED population, this trial suggests that incorporating a structured digital prompt like STIckER might be a worthwhile addition to your workflow. Remember that the benefit is tied to the tool's implementation, so ensure it integrates smoothly without creating undue burden on already busy staff. However, the evidence is based on this specific tool and population, so consider it an adjunct rather than a standalone protocol change.
articles.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of a digital patient decision aid, called STIckER, designed to boost rates of sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing among adolescents and young adults present…
articles.summary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of a digital patient decision aid, called STIckER, designed to boost rates of sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing among adolescents and young adults presenting to the emergency department. The core of the study was evaluating whether implementing this specific digital tool could practically improve current screening practices within the fast-paced ED environment. While the overall importance score was moderate, the design—a randomized controlled trial—lends weight to the findings regarding workflow integration and adherence to screening guidelines. It provides a real-world test of an intervention meant to address potential gaps in routine STI screening for this demographic.

articles.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
articles.takeaway: For patients with moderate-to-severe deficits from an acute middle-vessel occlusion, aiming for endovascular thrombectomy within 24 hours appears beneficial for functional independence. Remember that this benefit is specifically highlighted for this deficit severity and occlusion type. Proceed with standard workup, but keep the 24-hour window in mind for aggressive reperfusion strategies.
articles.shortSummary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO trial provides encouraging data suggesting that timely endovascular thrombectomy can improve functional outcomes in patients presenting with acute middle-vessel occlusion strokes who have moderate to s…
articles.summary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO trial provides encouraging data suggesting that timely endovascular thrombectomy can improve functional outcomes in patients presenting with acute middle-vessel occlusion strokes who have moderate to severe neurological deficits. Specifically, the analysis points toward improved functional independence when this intervention is performed within a 24-hour window from symptom onset. This is a valuable piece of information for guiding acute stroke management protocols, particularly in the setting of known or suspected middle-vessel occlusion. While the trial suggests a benefit, it's important to keep the context of the patient population and the timing of intervention in mind when applying these findings at the bedside.

articles.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
articles.takeaway: While the data suggests that minimizing time to resuscitation and on-scene treatment is beneficial for reducing mortality, remember that these associations might be confounded by patient severity. Therefore, focus on implementing system-level improvements to streamline care rather than solely interpreting time metrics in isolation. Always maintain a high index of suspicion for underlying systemic issues when interpreting delayed times.
articles.shortSummary: This multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS) analyzed how various prehospital time metrics relate to subsequent trauma outcomes.
articles.summary: This multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS) analyzed how various prehospital time metrics relate to subsequent trauma outcomes. The authors found a clear association where prolonged time to resuscitation (RT) and time to on-scene treatment (OST) were linked to higher mortality rates. Furthermore, extended OST and time to transport (TT) were associated with poorer functional outcomes. While these findings suggest that optimizing prehospital time is important for improving prognosis, the group acknowledged a critical limitation: these associations might simply reflect selection bias, meaning the most severely ill patients might inherently require more time and thus have worse outcomes regardless of the specific time metric.

articles.title: Major adverse cardiac events in patients with indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin testing and chest pain: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: When faced with an indeterminate hs-Tn in a chest pain patient, remember that the literature does not support a blanket assumption of increased MACE risk without serial testing. Focus heavily on a thorough clinical assessment, especially for those falling into low-risk categories, as this remains the most actionable step. Be cautious about interpreting these values in isolation.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review tackles the somewhat nebulous clinical scenario of patients presenting with chest pain and an indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin (hs-Tn) result.
articles.summary: This systematic review tackles the somewhat nebulous clinical scenario of patients presenting with chest pain and an indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin (hs-Tn) result. The authors conclude that there is currently insufficient evidence to definitively state whether these specific patients are at an increased risk for major adverse cardiac events (MACE). A key limitation highlighted is the inconsistent management of these indeterminate results across studies, with the review noting that a significant proportion of patients in the analyzed cohorts did not even receive serial troponin testing. Overall, the paper serves as a reminder that the interpretation of an indeterminate hs-Tn value requires significant clinical context rather than relying solely on the test result itself.

articles.title: High-Risk Medication Prescribing Among Older Adults in the Emergency Department: A National Assessment
articles.takeaway: Even with observed declines, remember that high-risk medication prescribing remains a significant issue in the ED setting for older adults. Pay particular attention to younger older adults and female patients, as they appear to be at higher risk. System-level checks, like mandatory pharmacist review or targeted discharge counseling, are warranted to mitigate these ongoing prescribing vulnerabilities.
articles.shortSummary: This national assessment looked at the prescribing patterns of high-risk medications, specifically those on the GEMS-Rx list, for older adults discharged from the emergency department between 2017 and 2022.
articles.summary: This national assessment looked at the prescribing patterns of high-risk medications, specifically those on the GEMS-Rx list, for older adults discharged from the emergency department between 2017 and 2022. The overall trend showed a decrease in the proportion of these high-risk fills, which is somewhat encouraging. However, the data revealed important nuances, noting that while the overall rate is still significant—nearly one in ten patients—younger older adults and female patients were disproportionately more likely to receive these medications upon discharge. The authors conclude that despite the general decline, this highlights persistent gaps in geriatric medication safety protocols that require targeted system-level improvements.

articles.title: Association between initial bandemia degree and short-term mortality among adults with bacteremia in the emergency department
articles.takeaway: When managing an adult with bacteremia in the ED, remember that a significantly elevated initial band count appears to be an independent marker associated with worse short-term outcomes. While this finding warrants consideration, do not let this single lab value overshadow the need for aggressive source control and appropriate empiric antibiotics. Always interpret this finding alongside clinical stability and source identification.
articles.shortSummary: This retrospective cohort study investigated whether the initial percentage of band neutrophils in adults presenting to the emergency department with confirmed bacteremia predicts short-term mortality.
articles.summary: This retrospective cohort study investigated whether the initial percentage of band neutrophils in adults presenting to the emergency department with confirmed bacteremia predicts short-term mortality. The authors found a statistically significant association, concluding that a higher initial band percentage is independently linked to an increased risk of death within 30 days. This suggests that beyond just the presence of bacteremia, the degree of left shift seen upon initial presentation might carry prognostic weight. While the study establishes this association, it is important to remember that this is observational data, and causality cannot be definitively established from this design alone. Nevertheless, it prompts a re-evaluation of routine laboratory findings in this high-risk population.

articles.title: Prehospital prediction of clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis: a prospective cohort study
articles.takeaway: While prehospital data like oxygen saturation, SBP, and qSOFA are associated with poor outcomes, remember that no single prehospital score predicts everything, and relying solely on these tools is insufficient. Use these initial findings to guide immediate resuscitation efforts, but do not let them replace the need for thorough in-hospital workup and established clinical judgment.
articles.shortSummary: This prospective cohort study analyzed prehospital data from 398 patients with suspected sepsis to see if initial field measurements could predict subsequent severe outcomes like septic shock or mortality.
articles.summary: This prospective cohort study analyzed prehospital data from 398 patients with suspected sepsis to see if initial field measurements could predict subsequent severe outcomes like septic shock or mortality. The authors found that several prehospital variables showed associations with sepsis severity and shock development, including oxygen saturation, systolic blood pressure, and qSOFA. Interestingly, the Glasgow Coma Score and male sex were linked to both severity and mortality, while specific findings like ashen skin or a focus of infection were associated with septic shock. While the study suggests prehospital scores might have some utility, it cautions that no single variable predicted all three measured outcomes, and the predictive power of any single tool is limited.

editions.label: 19 May 2026
editions.headline: CAP de-escalation, hyperkalaemia basics, and pragmatic updates across the ED
editions.summary: Today's set is strongest where it tightens common ED decisions rather than overturning them. The clearest practice signals are the 2025 CAP guideline update pushing shorter treatment and more selective antibiotics, the hyperkalaemia review reinforcing calcium-first management for unstable patients while moving older binders further out of the acute toolbox, and the pediatric sepsis fluid trial showing no clinically meaningful advantage for balanced fluid over saline. Around that core, several papers sharpen risk stratification and workflow: toxicology with a toxidrome-first framework, sedation safety, stroke selection, geriatric medication harm, and the limits of prehospital or biomarker shortcuts. Overall theme: be faster on the fundamentals, and more disciplined about where evidence does—or does not—justify escalation.
editions.notes: Remote repair applied after the local generator run failed to complete cleanly; core article selection and metadata were preserved.

articles.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
articles.takeaway: Revisit your default CAP pathway: use imaging deliberately, avoid automatic antibiotics when the clinical picture is convincingly viral, and think in terms of minimum-effective duration rather than fixed longer courses.
articles.shortSummary: The 2025 ATS update on CAP matters because it nudges ED care toward narrower, shorter treatment rather than reflexive broad coverage.
articles.summary: The 2025 ATS update on CAP matters because it nudges ED care toward narrower, shorter treatment rather than reflexive broad coverage. The practical signals are stronger support for expert-performed lung ultrasound, more willingness to withhold antibiotics when a convincing viral diagnosis is present in otherwise low-risk patients, and acceptance of shorter courses for stable non-severe disease.

articles.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
articles.takeaway: For dangerous hyperkalaemia, do the basics well: calcium for membrane stabilization, insulin-glucose plus salbutamol for shift, close glucose monitoring afterward, and early dialysis planning when the physiology is not going to cooperate.
articles.shortSummary: This review is a useful reset on acute hyperkalaemia: stabilize first, shift potassium second, and be honest about what actually works in the ED.
articles.summary: This review is a useful reset on acute hyperkalaemia: stabilize first, shift potassium second, and be honest about what actually works in the ED. Calcium remains the immediate priority when ECG change or marked hyperkalaemia is present, insulin-glucose still does most of the intracellular shifting, and inhaled beta-agonists are worth combining early. Older resin-based strategies continue to lose ground.

articles.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
articles.takeaway: In pediatric septic shock, either saline or balanced crystalloid is reasonable; focus your energy on early recognition, reassessment after each bolus, and rapid escalation when shock persists.
articles.shortSummary: PRoMPT BOLUS is valuable less for proving a winner than for removing a distraction.
articles.summary: PRoMPT BOLUS is valuable less for proving a winner than for removing a distraction. In children treated for septic shock, balanced crystalloid did not outperform 0.9% saline on major outcomes, which supports pragmatic local practice and keeps attention on timing, source control, vasoactive support, and overall resuscitation quality rather than fluid tribalism.

articles.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
articles.takeaway: For suspected LVO, let local transport times and thrombectomy access drive routing decisions; whichever pathway you use, protect imaging-to-reperfusion time as the metric that matters.
articles.shortSummary: This meta-analysis addresses a systems question every stroke network wrestles with: bypass to thrombectomy capability versus initial stabilization and transfer.
articles.summary: This meta-analysis addresses a systems question every stroke network wrestles with: bypass to thrombectomy capability versus initial stabilization and transfer. The literature remains heterogeneous, but the paper is still useful because it frames the tradeoff explicitly around time to reperfusion rather than institutional habit.

articles.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
articles.takeaway: When the toxin is unclear, build the case from syndrome, timeline, and expected trajectory, then get TOXBASE or poisons input early rather than waiting for diagnostic certainty.
articles.shortSummary: The RCEM/NPIS guideline is strong because it resists pretending unidentified poisoning can be reduced to one algorithm.
articles.summary: The RCEM/NPIS guideline is strong because it resists pretending unidentified poisoning can be reduced to one algorithm. Its value is in structuring first-pass ED care around toxidromes, toxicokinetics, and expected clinical evolution, while making clear that generic guidance should quickly hand off to agent-specific advice when available.

articles.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
articles.takeaway: In older patients, actively look for medication-caused or medication-worsened illness and use the ED visit to stop, simplify, or flag high-risk drugs where appropriate.
articles.shortSummary: This narrative review argues that medication harm in older adults is not a side issue in emergency care—it is part of the presenting illness burden.
articles.summary: This narrative review argues that medication harm in older adults is not a side issue in emergency care—it is part of the presenting illness burden. The practical contribution is a reminder that reconciliation, deprescribing, and pharmacist-supported review can be active ED interventions, not just ward tidy-up tasks.

articles.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
articles.takeaway: Ketamine remains a solid pediatric sedation option, but use this review as a prompt to tighten preparation, airway vigilance, and parent counseling rather than to become complacent because the drug is familiar.
articles.shortSummary: A large safety synthesis on pediatric IV ketamine is useful because ketamine remains common, familiar, and occasionally under-scrutinized.
articles.summary: A large safety synthesis on pediatric IV ketamine is useful because ketamine remains common, familiar, and occasionally under-scrutinized. Pooling adverse event rates and reported predictors helps calibrate risk more realistically than single-center anecdotes, even if prediction remains imperfect.

articles.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
articles.takeaway: If your service has a refractory VF protocol, vector change or DSED deserves serious consideration—but only within a rehearsed system that preserves compressions, charging workflow, and safety.
articles.shortSummary: This DOSE-VF secondary analysis supports the idea that refractory VF care may benefit from changing shock strategy rather than simply repeating standard defibrillation.
articles.summary: This DOSE-VF secondary analysis supports the idea that refractory VF care may benefit from changing shock strategy rather than simply repeating standard defibrillation. The mechanistic signal is shorter time spent in VF, especially with DSED, which is more clinically interesting than a purely theoretical impedance discussion.

articles.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
articles.takeaway: Do not let “minor” be the whole decision, but do require a clearly measurable disabling deficit before reaching for IV thrombolysis in borderline cases.
articles.shortSummary: The main contribution here is restraint.
articles.summary: The main contribution here is restraint. In minor stroke, the paper argues against treating every mild deficit as thrombolysis-worthy and instead pushes toward a more granular view of what counts as truly disabling.

articles.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
articles.takeaway: Use these data to examine where delay accumulates—scene, resuscitation, or transport—while avoiding simplistic blame based on total prehospital time alone.
articles.shortSummary: PATOS adds another reminder that prehospital time metrics matter, but interpretation is messy because long times often track with sicker patients.
articles.summary: PATOS adds another reminder that prehospital time metrics matter, but interpretation is messy because long times often track with sicker patients. The study is still useful for systems work because it separates components of delay rather than treating transport time as one undifferentiated number.

articles.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
articles.takeaway: If STI screening is inconsistent in your department, digital prompts or decision aids may be worth testing—but success will depend on workflow fit more than on the tool alone.
articles.shortSummary: This trial is less about STI testing itself than about whether patient-facing decision support can reliably move ED preventive care.
articles.summary: This trial is less about STI testing itself than about whether patient-facing decision support can reliably move ED preventive care. That makes it operationally interesting for departments trying to improve screening without relying entirely on clinician memory.

articles.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
articles.takeaway: For MeVO stroke with meaningful deficits, do not downplay the case as “too distal”; involve stroke and endovascular teams early and keep door-to-decision time tight.
articles.shortSummary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO signal is important because medium-vessel occlusion has been a gray zone between “definitely thrombectomy” and “best medical therapy.” A positive functional outcome signal is enough to keep this subgro…
articles.summary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO signal is important because medium-vessel occlusion has been a gray zone between “definitely thrombectomy” and “best medical therapy.” A positive functional outcome signal is enough to keep this subgroup on the front foot during stroke triage, even if external validation still matters.

articles.title: The prevalence of life-threatening diagnoses in emergency department patients with chest pain in a national group
articles.takeaway: Use prevalence data to steady your pre-test thinking, not to reassure yourself prematurely; ACS still dominates the dangerous end of the chest pain differential.
articles.shortSummary: This prevalence study is useful calibration.
articles.summary: This prevalence study is useful calibration. Most atraumatic chest pain is not catastrophic, but the serious-diagnosis rate is high enough that a casual or overly pathway-driven assessment still risks missing important disease.

articles.title: Major adverse cardiac events in patients with indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin testing and chest pain: a systematic review
articles.takeaway: If the troponin is indeterminate, default to serial interpretation and whole-patient risk assessment rather than forcing a decision from a single borderline result.
articles.shortSummary: Indeterminate hs-troponin results remain a workflow trap, and this review highlights how inconsistent current management still is.
articles.summary: Indeterminate hs-troponin results remain a workflow trap, and this review highlights how inconsistent current management still is. The key message is not that indeterminate values are benign, but that isolated numbers without serial context do not finish the job.

articles.title: Extracorporeal Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation for Pediatric Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest
articles.takeaway: For pediatric OHCA, follow local ECPR pathways if they exist, but do not over-interpret adult literature when counseling teams or families about expected benefit.
articles.shortSummary: Pediatric ECPR for OHCA remains a highly consequential but under-evidenced area.
articles.summary: Pediatric ECPR for OHCA remains a highly consequential but under-evidenced area. This paper is useful mainly because it makes the gap explicit: adult enthusiasm cannot simply be pasted onto children without stronger comparative data and mature local systems.

articles.title: High-Risk Medication Prescribing Among Older Adults in the Emergency Department: A National Assessment
articles.takeaway: Before discharging older adults, give new prescriptions the same scrutiny you give admission meds—especially sedatives, anticholinergics, and other familiar high-risk choices.
articles.shortSummary: This national assessment complements the broader geriatric medication review by showing that discharge prescribing risk remains common even as overall rates improve.
articles.summary: This national assessment complements the broader geriatric medication review by showing that discharge prescribing risk remains common even as overall rates improve. The important point is that progress has been partial and uneven, not solved.

articles.title: Association between initial bandemia degree and short-term mortality among adults with bacteremia in the emergency department
articles.takeaway: Marked bandemia in bacteremia should increase your vigilance and disposition caution, even if it does not independently dictate antibiotics or source-control decisions.
articles.shortSummary: Bandemia is not a magic marker, but this study suggests it still carries prognostic information in bacteremic adults.
articles.summary: Bandemia is not a magic marker, but this study suggests it still carries prognostic information in bacteremic adults. That matters less as a trigger for specific therapy and more as another signal against underestimating illness severity when the patient otherwise looks only moderately unwell.

articles.title: Clinical Features, CT Imaging Decisions and Yield by Age in Adults With Abdominal Pain in the Emergency Department
articles.takeaway: In older adults with abdominal pain, trust the benign-looking exam less and your imaging threshold less rigidly; age changes both presentation and test yield.
articles.shortSummary: This age-stratified abdominal pain paper reinforces a familiar bedside truth: exam findings get less trustworthy as patients get older, while imaging yield rises.
articles.summary: This age-stratified abdominal pain paper reinforces a familiar bedside truth: exam findings get less trustworthy as patients get older, while imaging yield rises. Its practical value is in legitimizing a lower threshold for CT in selected older adults rather than pretending one abdominal pain heuristic fits all ages.

articles.title: Prehospital prediction of clinical course in patients with suspected sepsis: a prospective cohort study
articles.takeaway: Use prehospital sepsis scores to heighten attention and resource planning, not to substitute for repeated bedside reassessment once the patient hits the ED.
articles.shortSummary: This prehospital sepsis cohort is a good argument against score worship.
articles.summary: This prehospital sepsis cohort is a good argument against score worship. qSOFA and other variables carry some signal, but no single tool reliably predicts severity, shock, and mortality together, which is exactly what frontline clinicians often wish they would do.

articles.title: Civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury in South-East Norway
articles.takeaway: For potentially salvageable penetrating TBI, the challenge is often protocol execution rather than protocol invention: image early, coordinate neurosurgery fast, and close obvious compliance gaps.
articles.shortSummary: The Norway pTBI series is less about changing first principles than about exposing implementation gaps.
articles.summary: The Norway pTBI series is less about changing first principles than about exposing implementation gaps. Survivors can do well, but only if teams actually deliver the aggressive protocolized care they claim to believe in—antibiotics, ICP strategy, vascular imaging, and timely operative review.

editions.label: 18 May 2026
editions.headline: Fluid Choice, Pneumonia Stewardship, and Hyperkalemia: Key Updates for Today's ED
editions.summary:
The evidence base is shifting rapidly across several core ED domains this week. On the resuscitation front, the PRoMPT BOLUS trial offers a reassuring message for pediatric septic shock: the choice between 0.9% saline and balanced crystalloids appears equally effective regarding major endpoints, allowing for greater flexibility in fluid stewardship. Meanwhile, managing acute hyperkalemia remains high-stakes; the consensus reinforces that calcium administration is the immediate priority if ECG changes are present, while cautioning against relying on older agents like sodium polystyrene sulfonate.

In infectious disease, the updated 2025 CAP guidelines signal a move toward aggressive antibiotic stewardship, suggesting that for otherwise healthy patients with positive viral testing, antibiotics may be unnecessary, and shortening therapy to a minimum of three days is now favored. Furthermore, when faced with an unknown toxin, the emphasis is on a structured, toxidromic approach rather than a single diagnostic pathway. These updates, alongside reminders to systematically deprescribe medications in our geriatric patients, underscore a theme of precision: optimizing the intervention based on the most current, nuanced data.

Keep these guidelines in mind to refine your initial workup and de-escalate care where appropriate.
editions.notes: Today’s reading set emphasizes targeted, evidence-based adjustments rather than broad protocol overhauls.

articles.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
articles.takeaway: When managing stable CAP, remember the updated emphasis on de-escalation; consider a minimum 3-day antibiotic course for both outpatient and nonsevere inpatient settings. Also, don't overlook the role of lung ultrasound in the ED, and be mindful that positive viral testing alone may negate the need for antibiotics in otherwise healthy patients.
articles.shortSummary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines offer an update on the diagnosis and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), which remains a frequent encounter in the ED.
articles.summary: The new 2025 American Thoracic Society guidelines offer an update on the diagnosis and management of community-acquired pneumonia (CAP), which remains a frequent encounter in the ED. These updated recommendations touch upon imaging modalities, antibiotic stewardship, and the role of corticosteroids. Notably, the guidelines seem to bolster the utility of lung ultrasound when performed by experienced personnel, suggesting it's a valuable tool for ED assessment. Furthermore, there's a shift in thinking regarding antibiotic necessity, suggesting that for otherwise healthy patients with positive viral testing, antibiotics might be unnecessary. Clinically, the guidelines advocate for shortening the duration of antibiotic therapy to a minimum of three days for both stable outpatients and those admitted with nonsevere CAP.

articles.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
articles.takeaway: For acute management, remember that calcium is the priority if the patient has ECG changes or K+ ≥ 6.5 mmol/L. Use insulin-glucose combined with inhaled beta-agonists for shifting potassium, but be vigilant for subsequent hypoglycemia. Do not rely on sodium polystyrene sulfonate, and treat newer binders as adjuncts pending more acute care data.
articles.shortSummary: This review synthesizes the current evidence base for managing acute hyperkalemia in the emergency department, highlighting that prompt recognition is key to preventing life-threatening arrhythmias.
articles.summary: This review synthesizes the current evidence base for managing acute hyperkalemia in the emergency department, highlighting that prompt recognition is key to preventing life-threatening arrhythmias. The core management strategy revolves around stabilizing the myocardium with intravenous calcium salts if ECG changes are present or if potassium levels reach ≥6.5 mmol/L. Insulin-glucose therapy remains a primary method for shifting potassium intracellularly, and its efficacy is enhanced by adding inhaled beta-agonists like salbutamol. Importantly, the guidelines explicitly de-emphasize older agents, noting that sodium polystyrene sulfonate is no longer recommended due to safety concerns. While newer binders like sodium zirconium cyclosilicate are promising, the authors caution that they lack sufficient validation in the acute care setting, reserving dialysis for refractory instances.

articles.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
articles.takeaway: When managing pediatric septic shock, the choice between 0.9% saline and balanced crystalloids appears equally effective regarding major endpoints like kidney injury and mortality. You can confidently use either fluid type without worrying about compromising care based on fluid composition alone. However, remember that this trial only addresses fluid choice and does not negate the need for timely source control or vasopressors if shock persists.
articles.shortSummary: This large, pragmatic randomized trial, PRoMPT BOLUS, directly addresses the long-standing debate regarding the optimal crystalloid fluid choice for pediatric septic shock resuscitation by comparing 0.9% saline against…
articles.summary: This large, pragmatic randomized trial, PRoMPT BOLUS, directly addresses the long-standing debate regarding the optimal crystalloid fluid choice for pediatric septic shock resuscitation by comparing 0.9% saline against balanced crystalloids. The study's primary strength lies in its real-world applicability across multiple pediatric networks. The key finding is quite definitive: there was no statistically significant difference in major clinical outcomes, including kidney injury and overall mortality, when using either 0.9% saline or balanced crystalloids. This provides robust evidence that fluid type, within the context of resuscitation, does not dictate superior outcomes in this critically ill pediatric population. This is a major piece of evidence for guiding fluid stewardship in the ED.

articles.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
articles.takeaway: For AIS-LVO, the evidence synthesis suggests that the optimal transfer pathway remains debated, but the review provides a quantitative comparison of outcomes between direct transport versus local evaluation first. While the meta-analysis is helpful, remember that institutional protocols and local resource availability must guide decision-making, and the authors caution that the evidence base itself is varied. Always consider the time-sensitive nature of reperfusion therapy when choosing a pathway.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the persistent debate surrounding the best out-of-hospital referral pathway for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke secondary to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-…
articles.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis tackles the persistent debate surrounding the best out-of-hospital referral pathway for patients presenting with acute ischemic stroke secondary to a large vessel occlusion (AIS-LVO). Specifically, it compares the outcomes of the 'mothership' approach, which involves direct transport to a comprehensive stroke center, against the 'drip-and-ship' strategy, which entails initial evaluation at a local facility before transfer. The authors synthesize existing data to provide a comparative effectiveness assessment of these two established protocols. Given the high stakes in managing AIS-LVO, understanding which transfer model optimizes time to definitive care is crucial for improving patient outcomes.

articles.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
articles.takeaway: When faced with an unidentified poison, adopt a structured toxidromic approach, focusing heavily on the patient's clinical course and the predicted toxicokinetics of potential agents. Remember that this guideline is a scaffold, not a replacement; always defer to the specific, detailed guidance provided by TOXBASE or your local poisons centre for definitive management steps. Keep the initial assessment systematic to guide your differential diagnosis.
articles.shortSummary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a comprehensive, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED when poisoning is suspec…
articles.summary: This new joint guideline from the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and the National Poisons Information Service provides a comprehensive, generalized framework for managing patients in the ED when poisoning is suspected but the agent remains unidentified. The core concept revolves around adopting a toxidromic approach, meaning the management strategy must be guided by the patient's evolving clinical picture and the known toxicokinetics of potential poisons. It outlines a structured process for initial assessment, helping the clinician systematically narrow down the differential diagnosis of ingested toxins. Crucially, the authors emphasize that this guideline is a general support tool and explicitly states it does not supersede the detailed, agent-specific advice found in TOXBASE or the local poisons centre protocols. This is a valuable resource for standardizing initial management in the face of diagnostic uncertainty.

articles.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
articles.takeaway: When managing an older patient in the ED, make medication reconciliation a non-negotiable step, focusing specifically on deprescribing potentially inappropriate agents using validated tools. Don't just document the list; actively challenge the necessity of every drug. Remember that pharmacist consultation is a high-yield resource here, so utilize it early in the workup.
articles.shortSummary: This narrative review tackles the critical issue of medication safety in the elderly patient presenting to the emergency department, recognizing that polypharmacy and medication exposure are major sources of risk in thi…
articles.summary: This narrative review tackles the critical issue of medication safety in the elderly patient presenting to the emergency department, recognizing that polypharmacy and medication exposure are major sources of risk in this increasingly common demographic. Given that older adults constitute a significant portion of ED visits and present with complex comorbidities, optimizing medication regimens is paramount for improving outcomes. The authors emphasize that systematic medication reconciliation and the proactive deprescribing of potentially inappropriate medications using established tools like Beers or STOPP/START criteria are key interventions. Furthermore, they highlight that integrating dedicated emergency medicine pharmacist support and leveraging clinical decision support systems can significantly bolster safety protocols within the fast-paced ED environment.

articles.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
articles.takeaway: When considering ketamine for pediatric procedural sedation, remember that this review aggregates adverse event frequencies and potential predictors, offering a broader view than any single study. While it's useful for understanding overall risk profiles, be mindful that the clinical importance of the identified predictors is estimated and may not translate perfectly to your specific patient population. Always maintain a high index of suspicion for adverse reactions, regardless of the pooled data.
articles.shortSummary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department.
articles.summary: This systematic review synthesizes the existing literature regarding the use of intravenous ketamine for procedural sedation in the pediatric emergency department. The primary goal was to consolidate the reported rates of adverse events associated with this practice across multiple studies, specifically focusing on those involving a substantial number of subjects, ideally ≥250. Beyond just compiling adverse event frequencies, the authors also took the time to inventory the predictor variables that have been described in the literature and attempt to estimate their actual clinical weight. This synthesis is valuable because it provides a consolidated view of safety profiles and risk stratification factors that might otherwise be scattered across disparate case reports and smaller studies.

articles.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
articles.takeaway: The data suggest that minimizing the time spent in VF between shocks is a critical target for improving OHCA outcomes. While DSED appears most promising in this analysis, remember that this is a secondary review of existing trial data, not a definitive recommendation. Clinically, this reinforces the need for rapid, optimized defibrillation protocols rather than just increasing the number of shocks.
articles.shortSummary: This review delves into the defibrillation data from the DOSE-VF Trial, specifically examining whether shortening the duration of ventricular fibrillation (VF) between shocks is the mechanism driving improved survival i…
articles.summary: This review delves into the defibrillation data from the DOSE-VF Trial, specifically examining whether shortening the duration of ventricular fibrillation (VF) between shocks is the mechanism driving improved survival in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The analysis compared different defibrillation approaches, including Double Sequential Defibrillation (DSED) and Vector Change (VC) defibrillation, against standard protocols. The key finding suggests that both DSED and VC strategies are associated with a reduction in the time spent in VF compared to standard care, with DSED showing the most encouraging signal. This shifts the focus from simply delivering more shocks to optimizing the timing and sequence of electrical therapy to minimize VF duration.

articles.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
articles.takeaway: Unless the patient presents with a clearly demonstrable and measurable deficit, withholding IVT for minor stroke is the safer default. Don't treat all minor deficits as indications for thrombolysis; reserve this intervention for clear, significant impairment. Remember that current evidence suggests a need for more targeted trials before changing standard practice.
articles.shortSummary: This piece tackles the often murky area of managing minor stroke presentations with intravenous thrombolytics (IVT).
articles.summary: This piece tackles the often murky area of managing minor stroke presentations with intravenous thrombolytics (IVT). The core message is a strong caution against the routine use of IVT in patients presenting with only minor deficits. The authors suggest that future randomized controlled trials need to be more granular, specifically investigating which types of neurological deficits might actually benefit from thrombolytic therapy. Overall, the consensus leans toward reserving this potent intervention for those with clear, measurable, and significant impairment, rather than offering it broadly to all minor stroke patients.

articles.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
articles.takeaway: When reviewing prehospital metrics, remember that prolonged RT and OST correlate with worse outcomes, suggesting a push to expedite care. However, be mindful that this association might simply reflect that the sickest patients take longer to manage. Therefore, use these metrics to guide system improvements rather than solely judging individual care times.
articles.shortSummary: This multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS) analyzed how various prehospital time metrics relate to subsequent trauma outcomes.
articles.summary: This multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS) analyzed how various prehospital time metrics relate to subsequent trauma outcomes. The authors found a clear association where prolonged time to resuscitation (RT) and time to on-scene treatment (OST) were linked to higher mortality rates. Furthermore, extended OST and time to transport (TT) were associated with poorer functional outcomes. While these findings strongly suggest that optimizing prehospital time is beneficial, the authors caution that these associations might also be confounded by the fact that the most severely ill patients inherently require more time and intervention.

articles.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
articles.takeaway: If your department struggles with routine STI screening in this demographic, implementing a standardized, easily accessible digital prompt like STIckER warrants consideration. Remember that the tool acts as a structured reminder, but it does not replace clinical judgment; always ensure the prompt is integrated smoothly into the existing workflow to maximize adherence without causing undue friction. Be mindful that its efficacy is tied to proper implementation and provider buy-in.
articles.shortSummary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of a digital patient decision aid, called STIckER, designed to boost rates of sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing among adolescents and young adults present…
articles.summary: This randomized controlled trial assessed the utility of a digital patient decision aid, called STIckER, designed to boost rates of sexually transmitted infection (STI) testing among adolescents and young adults presenting to the emergency department. The core question was whether integrating such a tool into the ED workflow could practically improve screening compliance. The study design is appropriate for testing intervention efficacy in a real-world setting, which is valuable for process improvement in high-volume departments.
